question: Define an open set in a metric space (X, d). Provide the formal definition and explain it in your own words.
expectedPoints: State the formal definition: A set U ⊆ X is open if for every x ∈ U, there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is contained in U.
Explain that every point in an open set has a 'neighborhood' or 'bubble' of space around it that is also entirely within the set.
Contrast this with a set that is not open, where a point on the boundary would have no such neighborhood entirely within the set.

question: Prove that the union of any collection of open sets in a metric space is itself an open set.
expectedPoints: Begin the proof: Let {U_i}_{i ∈ I} be an arbitrary collection of open sets, and let U = ∪_{i ∈ I} U_i.
Take an arbitrary point x ∈ U. By definition of union, x ∈ U_j for some specific j in the index set I.
Since U_j is open, there exists an ε > 0 such that B(x, ε) ⊆ U_j.
Since U_j ⊆ U, it follows that B(x, ε) ⊆ U. Because x was arbitrary, this proves U is open.

question: Let (X, d) be a metric space. Prove that a set F ⊆ X is closed if and only if its complement, X \ F, is open. Use this theorem to show that the intersection of any finite collection of open sets is open, but that this property may fail for an infinite intersection.
expectedPoints: Prove the theorem: First, assume F is closed (contains all its limit points) and show its complement is open (for any point not in F, you can find a ball around it that also avoids F). Then, assume the complement is open and show F must contain all its limit points.

Use the theorem and De Morgan's laws: The complement of a finite intersection of open sets is a finite union of closed sets. Since finite unions of closed sets are closed, the original finite intersection must be open.
Provide a counterexample for the infinite case, e.g., in R with the standard metric, the infinite intersection of open sets (-1/n, 1/n) for n ∈ N is the singleton {0}, which is not open.
